entries.summary: Has been immensely beneficial in creating a unified repository for all our work-related information
entries.review_text:
What is our primary use case?

I have a genuine fondness for this tool. It's user-friendly and has been immensely beneficial in creating a unified repository for all our work-related information. Whether it's vendor selection procedures or simplifying our lives through data analysis and comparisons, it undeniably adds substantial value to our work, aiding us in strategic decision-making.


What is most valuable?

In the process of obtaining representative sites in NaviNet, it assists us in facilitating decision-making and taking action.


What needs improvement?

It's challenging due to the frequent changes, but I believe most of these changes align with our company's evolving needs. Our organization is constantly changing, requiring new fields and information. Sometimes, when you access the platform, you'll encounter new mandatory fields. I'm confident that these changes are driven by our company's requests, rather than being under the control of the vendor provider. My only suggestion might be to include a brief description of any new fields we introduce, making it easier for people to use them. Perhaps this is something they can consider implementing. They can also include training whenever there is a new update.


For how long have I used the solution?

I have been using Workday for the past three years.  


What do I think about the stability of the solution?

It is stable. 


How are customer service and support?

The support is excellent. 


How was the initial setup?

 The initial setup is easy and I rate it a 10 out of 10.


What was our ROI?

I would say that it has indeed yielded a good return on investment. Of course, one thing I can share is that during our vendor selection process, a part of the criteria we consider is affordability. I may not have the exact figures regarding the price compared to their competitors, but I can confirm that our procurement process takes pricing into account. It's likely a reasonable price, perhaps not the lowest, but certainly a fair one.


What other advice do I have?

Like any other software system, the initial setup is crucial, and it requires a degree of follow-up and monitoring in the early stages. However, it proves to be an effective and valuable solution in the end.  I would rate it a nine out of ten.

entries.summary: Enables seamless organization and interface customization while being easy to use
entries.review_text:
What is our primary use case?

Communication, stakeholder management, and engagement are ideal use cases for this solution.


What is most valuable?

Arranging several subsections within a section is easy by left-clicking and moving them around with the mouse. I can organize the interface the way I want, and everything I get is adaptable.


What needs improvement?

It is easy to use, and I love its UI. The only problem I experience is that it often hangs. I might find myself trying to load a new page, but I am unable to move from one page to another due to frequent hanging.


For how long have I used the solution?

I have used Notion for project management over the past year.


What do I think about the stability of the solution?

I'd rate its stability at eight out of ten. During the day, especially around eight PM GMT plus three hours, there's a long loading period for content. While incidents happen three times a week at most, they usually occur once weekly.


How are customer service and support?

Customer support is great, effective, and easy to reach. I would give it a rating of ten out of ten.

How would you rate customer service and support?

Positive

Which solution did I use previously and why did I switch?

I tried using another solution, however, I didn't like its UI feature. Compared to Notion, it required too many clicks, so I left it alone.


What's my experience with pricing, setup cost, and licensing?

It's affordable and offers community support if I get stuck. I can find help through community forums like Google, where people share insights on navigating problems. Many people understand it, offering useful feedback from various HITRID.


Which other solutions did I evaluate?

I don't use alternate solutions.


What other advice do I have?

The decision to use this tool was solely mine. I appreciate its friendly UI and monochrome colors, which avoid excessive color mixing. This feature and the ease of navigation have kept me committed. I can understand it quickly compared to other tools.  I rate the overall solution eight out of ten; it is a basket of convenience.

entries.summary: Excellent CRM for a sales team, with improve areas.
entries.review_text:
Use Cases: Close (CRM) is used to support our sales team, we can handle several options like emails, messages, and call directly to our future clients, keeping continuous communication with them. Our big trouble with this CRM (Close) is tracking hours from our sales team, this CRM is really good to increase sales and having a great sales interaction and follow-up, but, is a headache to track hours, breaks, lunch from the team, and we must implement a special software to track and reports hours from agents.

### Pros and Cons

Pros: - Excellent to handle email with customer.
- We can implement links to fill out some PCI Complaint form.
- Holistic Reports of sales and agents follow up.
- Pretty good price per user and different levels of services.

Cons: - Hours tracking, must improve in get and run data hours.
- Reports could improve, be more detailed and specific.
- Predictive dialer just added in more expensive plan.


Edit ProsEdit Cons

### Return on Investment
- Excellent cost/benefit performance
- Economic price and options provided accordingly
- Good management of the sales team and its performance


### Alternatives Considered
- Aspect Unified IP, RingCentral Contact Center and Five9


For a specific sales team, Close performs better sales tracking and email and SMS management, with a fresh and modern interface, making the team's work more comfortable, with a holistic (but not complete) tracking and reporting of the data. Helping to improve the performance of each agent and obtain better results. For all other campaigns we use the predictive dialers mentioned above, Close is better for tracking current customers and for warm leads.

### Key Insights

#### Do you think Close CRM delivers good value for the price?

Yes

#### Are you happy with Close CRM's feature set?

No

#### Did Close CRM live up to sales and marketing promises?

Yes

#### Did implementation of Close CRM go as expected?

Yes

#### Would you buy Close CRM again?

Yes

RingCentral Contact Center, RingCentral MVP, Five9, Aspect Unified IP, Slack, Cisco Jabber, Clockify, LiveVox CCaaS

### Usability

8

It is a very good and almost complete software (CRM), the easy and direct handling of different ways of communicating with clients is really good, it has areas for improvement like everything in life, they can improve the reporting area and offer a better Experience in managing agent hours and tracking them.

### Support Rating

9

Close's support team offers a really good service, they are always willing to help with whatever is needed or arises, they investigate everything they need to find a solution and give an answer as quickly as possible, their attitude is better and their optimism really helps in times of stress, they do a great job in record time.

### Likelihood to Recommend

Excellent software to increase sales and follow up with existing and new customers. Email and SMS handling are really easy for the sales team, managing leads is really great, and Close merges duplicate leads to help our Data Leads avoid repetitive and unnecessary calls. As mentioned in some comments before, must improve in reports, Close is not appropriate to handle or track agents' hours making it necessary to implement additional software to cover this area.

entries.review_text: What do you like best about Jira?I like best about Jira is its strong issue tracking and workflow customization. As a QA professional, it gives me complete visibility into bug status, priorities, sprint progress, and release tracking. The ability to create detailed tickets with attachments, logs, steps to reproduce, and custom fields makes communication with developers very clear. I also appreciate how workflows can be tailored to match our testing and release process. Dashboards and filters help me quickly track open defects and monitor sprint quality, which makes daily tracking structured and efficient.Review collected by and hosted on G2.com.What do you dislike about Jira?One thing I dislike about Jira is that it can sometimes feel complex and heavy, especially for new users. The interface has many options, and without proper configuration, it can become overwhelming. Advanced filters and JQL are powerful, but they have a learning curve. Performance can also slow down in large projects with many custom fields and workflows. While it is very flexible, that flexibility sometimes makes setup and maintenance more time consuming than expectedReview collected by and hosted on G2.com.

entries.summary: A very user-friendly tool for HR-related activities requiring an easy setup process
entries.review_text:
What is our primary use case?

I use BambooHR for everything related to HR activities, like maintaining the data of all our employees or for documentation sharing between our colleagues and us. BambooHR's homepage is also very useful since one can add links over there, and everyone can access it in one go. Also, the solution's onboarding and offboarding sections are important since we use them quite a lot for managing vacation and time off requests. Now, we are thinking about using the asset parts of BambooHR.


What needs improvement?

There are some issues with the job tab under my info where you add the salary. So, if you add the monthly salary, for example, because this is what we're using. It's a custom in our country to refer to it as the monthly amount and not the annual amount, but sometimes I need it. If they had this calculated automatically, it would help a lot. If you entered the annual amount, you would have to calculate it yourself and then insert the annual amount. However, if I insert the monthly amount, it would be helpful if the system would give me the annual amount automatically. Also, the solution should be made globally available. 


For how long have I used the solution?

I have been using BambooHR for about three years now. Also, I don't remember the version of the solution I am using. I am a customer of the solution. 


What do I think about the stability of the solution?

I rate the solution's stability a nine out of ten since sometimes it has little delay or lag.


How are customer service and support?

The solution's technical support is super responsive and helpful. I rate the technical support a ten out of ten.

How would you rate customer service and support?

Positive

Which solution did I use previously and why did I switch?

I have previously worked with Namely and NetSuite. NetSuite was more for accounting purposes.


How was the initial setup?

The solution's initial setup process is easy.


What's my experience with pricing, setup cost, and licensing?

Price-wise, the solution is in the middle compared to other tools offering similar functionalities. 


What other advice do I have?

The solution can be useful for mid-sized companies and not enterprise-sized companies. BambooHR is user-friendly, the setup process is easy, and their support team is super responsive since they give you the exact steps and very comprehensive instructions with which you can easily modify or change. Since the solution is not quite global and is very specific to North America, especially the US, and not quite popular in Europe, I rate the overall solution an eight out of ten.

entries.summary: Powerful visualization with strong enterprise adoption.
entries.review_text:
My experience with Tableau has been exceptional. This is particularly true for data visualization and business intelligence use cases that require fast insights and strong visual storytelling. Tableau is well suited for organizations that want to empower business users while still supporting advanced analytical needs. It performs reliable at scale and is widely adopted across teams once deployed.

What I like: Tableau's visualization capabilities are its biggest strength. It enables users to quickly build intuitive and interactive dashboards with minimal development effort. Its flexibility to connect to a wide range of data sources and ability to explore data visually make it a strong tool for self-service analytics. Performance is generally solid, even with complex dashboards. 

What I dislike: Licensing can become expensive as usage grows, especially in large enterprises with many viewers and creators. Governance and content management require careful planning to avoid dashboard issues. Some advanced use cases still require strong data modeling and backend preparation to ensure consistency.

entries.summary: Shopify with Magento features comparison
entries.review_text:
Hi all.

this is a cross-post (similar one posted in Magento reddit), but I have good excuse for that in this situation.

Our clients and prospects often ask about the differences between Magento and Shopify. There are lots of high-level comparisons around, but I couldn't find anything very detailed, down to specific features.

So I decided to build my own and made a quite extensive feature comparison list of Magento vs Shopify.

It is available as Google Spreadsheet at [this link](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1OQYtfTHKy7T0UzT-y9xmOTVRLV17Zd2gvQumb2MqckI/edit#gid=1241163601)

What I thought next is that this comparison may benefit from input of other people, because with all my best efforts, I may miss something or did something wrong. Nobody is perfect.

Hence I made this spreadsheet open for edits, and encourage everybody to contribute.

Otherwise you are just welcome to use it when make decision what platform to use or advise your clients on that.

P.S.  I admit that the comparison is a bit Magento-centric, Magento features list was taken as a starting point and I know this platform better.

entries.summary: Woocommerce Admin -> Edit order -> Prices change on update
entries.review_text:
I have a tough topic.

I integrate multiple channels so that orders from all of them appear in my Woocommerce store. For some of these orders prices of the products are higher than their regular price in the store. Now whenever I make any change to such order in Woocommerce, prices of all the products are updated to the store regular price. This causes a terrible mess in the order and there is no way to undo it.

Does anyone know how to prevent it? Maybe allowing for prices higher than regular ones could also solve it? Just dunno how to do it :(

Thanks a lot for any assistance.

entries.summary: bug: post-call Gmail draft + Salesforce sync buttons have no loading state or error feedback
entries.review_text:
## Problem

The post-call panel buttons (`Create Draft` and `Sync to Salesforce`) fire async operations with no visual feedback — no spinner, no disabled state, no success/error message in the overlay.

**File:** `content/orum-overlay.js` lines 722–723

```js
content.querySelector('#sdrc-create-draft-btn')?.addEventListener('click', () => triggerDraftEmail(callRecord));
content.querySelector('#sdrc-sync-sf-btn')?.addEventListener('click', () => triggerSalesforceSyncUI(callRecord));
```

Also in `dashboard/dashboard.js` lines 465–467, success/failure is surfaced via browser `alert()` dialogs — jarring UX on a tool SDRs use during live calls.

## Impact

- User double-clicks (thinks it didn't work) → duplicate Salesforce tasks and Gmail drafts
- No way to tell if sync succeeded without opening Gmail/Salesforce
- `alert()` dialogs interrupt flow and feel unpolished

## Fix

1. Disable button + show spinner on click
2. On success: show inline ✓ confirmation that auto-clears after 3s
3. On failure: show inline red error message, re-enable button
4. Replace `alert()` in dashboard with toast notifications

entries.summary: Copper keepout under antenna is too small
entries.review_text:
## Severity
Must-fix

## Location
PCB layout – antenna area

## Problem
The copper-free area under the antenna appears to be too small. From the project requirements, the PCB does not need to be physically cut under the antenna, but the copper underneath it must be cleared properly. In the current layout, that keepout area is not large enough.

## Why it matters
Leaving too much copper under or too close to the antenna can detune it and reduce RF performance, which may affect communication range and overall wireless reliability.

## Suggested fix
Please increase the copper keepout area under the antenna so that no copper pour or routed signals remain in the required antenna clearance zone, and re-check the layout against the antenna placement recommendations.

entries.summary: Why can Zoom re-trigger Chrome's Mic prompt while my Web App stays "Silent" after being Blocked?
entries.review_text:
Why can Zoom re-trigger Chrome's Mic prompt while my Web App stays "Silent" after being Blocked?

I'm facing a frustrating UX issue with microphone permissions in Chrome.

The Flow: User grants mic access -> User later goes to Chrome Site Settings and manually toggles the Microphone to "Off" (Denied).

The Issue: In my app, calling navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia results in an immediate NotAllowedError. No native browser UI or prompt is shown to the user.

The Mystery: I noticed that Zoom and Google Meet can somehow re-trigger the native "Permission Blocked" bubble or prompt even when the site is manually blocked in settings.



I suspect there is a hidden 'Super-User' Web API or a specific Manifest flag that only 'Trusted Partners' like Zoom can use to force-reset the denied state. Since chrome.contentSettings is restricted to extensions, is Google intentionally gatekeeping the PermissionReset function from standard web apps to maintain their dominance in the meeting space?

entries.summary: AVCaptureVideoDataOutput stops zooming while AVCaptureVideoPreviewLayer continues — physical wide / ultra-wide / telephoto only
entries.review_text:
AVCaptureVideoDataOutput stops zooming while AVCaptureVideoPreviewLayer continues — physical wide / ultra-wide / telephoto only

We use a single AVCaptureSession with AVCaptureVideoPreviewLayer and AVCaptureVideoDataOutput (preview-sized buffers, BGRA).
When we increase videoZoomFactor, beyond a certain zoom level the image from AVCaptureVideoDataOutput no longer zooms further, while AVCaptureVideoPreviewLayer continues to zoom with the same zoom control. The preview and the video-data output therefore diverge.
This behavior appears when the active camera is a physical lens device — wide, ultra-wide, or telephoto (e.g. builtInWideAngleCamera, builtInUltraWideCamera, builtInTelephotoCamera, or similar). It does not appear when the active input is a virtual / multi-camera (e.g. triple camera, dual-wide, or other system multi-camera).
Are there known conditions under which this mismatch between preview and video-data output is expected?
Thank you.

entries.summary: [BUG] Idle workers report ghost commit hashes (never pushed, never PR'd)
entries.review_text:
## Constat (2026-04-18, audit intercom vs PR par coordinateur ai-01)

Plusieurs messages intercom de cette semaine annoncent des commits avec hash précis, **mais ces hashes n'existent nulle part** après \`git fetch --all\` sur parent + submod :

| Message | Machine | Hash annoncé | Travail | État |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 07:19Z | po-2023 | \`f5345b88\` (submod) + \`9f4371e7\` (parent) | JsonCsvExporter.ts — 5 tests | ❌ introuvable |
| 14:33Z | ai-01 (worker) | \`71a549e0\` | CacheAntiLeakManager.test.ts — 24 tests | ❌ introuvable |
| 16:35Z | po-2024 (worker idle) | \`71a849e0\` (typo du précédent ?) | Même test, même module | ❌ introuvable |

\`git cat-file -t <hash>\` retourne \`fatal: Not a valid object name\` sur les 4 hashes, sur parent ET submod, après \`fetch --all\`.

## Interprétations possibles

1. **Agents fabriquent les hashes** dans leurs rapports. Ils décrivent un travail plausible (tests ajoutés, coverage améliorée) mais n'ont pas réellement committé, ou le report est posté avant \`git commit\`.
2. **Commits existent brièvement puis sont supprimés** par un cleanup prématuré (\`Remove-Worktree\`, \`Remove-RemoteBranch\`, ou guard \`Test-OnlyAutoCommits\` qui fire à tort).
3. **Les hashes sont corrompus** dans la rédaction des rapports (ex : \`71a549e0\` vs \`71a849e0\` — typo confirmée sur le même travail).

## Impact

User a exprimé (2026-04-18) : **"Je ne veux pas que les agents travaillent dans le vide"**.

Or ici : les idle workers affirment avoir fait du travail, leurs reports passent sur le dashboard et dans les inbox, le coordinateur planifie en supposant que ce travail existe — mais il n'existe pas. C'est du **faux progrès** qui :
- Saturate le dashboard sans valeur
- Détourne le coordinateur (j'ai cherché à créer un PR pour du travail fantôme)
- Ne donne aucune amélioration réelle de la couverture de tests
- Masque le vrai problème (workers qui échouent silencieusement)

## Investigation suggérée

1. **Vérifier le prompt idle** (\`scripts/scheduling/start-claude-worker.ps1:2766-2794\`) — exige-t-il explicitement un \`git log -1\` APRÈS le commit pour que l'agent remonte le vrai hash ?
2. **Logger les post-commit checks** — le worker script devrait capturer le hash réel post-commit via \`git log -1 --format=%H\` et l'inclure dans le Mark-TaskAsComplete, pas laisser l'agent l'auto-déclarer.
3. **Audit du guard \`Test-OnlyAutoCommits\`** (ligne 1634) — si un commit idle \`test(coverage):\` a autre chose dans son log (ex : un auto-commit préalable + le vrai commit), le guard fire-t-il et supprime-t-il tout ?
4. **Audit cross-machine** : combien de ghost hashes dans les 30 derniers rapports ?

## Corrections candidates (harness)

- **Option A (faible coût)** : modifier le prompt idle pour exiger l'output de \`git log -1 --format="%H %s"\` comme **preuve** du commit. Si absent, le worker script rejette le report avec STATUS: failure.
- **Option B (plus robuste)** : le worker script lui-même parse \`git log main..HEAD\` avant Mark-TaskAsComplete et remplit les hashes réels dans le rapport, **ignorant** ce que l'agent déclare.
- **Option C (drastique)** : désactiver le mode idle coverage jusqu'à fix. Les workers restent dormants si pas de dispatch, plutôt que de produire du bruit fantôme.

## Ne fait PAS partie de cette issue

- Réparer rétroactivement les tests annoncés mais non committés — si la coverage en a vraiment besoin, un dispatch explicite vaut mieux.
- Auditer tous les idle reports historiques.

## Labels

bug, claude-only (fix dans \`scripts/scheduling/start-claude-worker.ps1\`, harnais)

Refs : cycle coordination 2026-04-18 18:00Z, audit intercom explicite demandé par utilisateur.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Struggling to diagnose this as it happens so infrequently, a couple of times a day my game will freeze and crash while running photon-main (running voxy so the modrinth version doesn't work).

entries.summary: AADSTS500200 error when using personal Microsoft account to grant access to Azure Resource Manager
entries.review_text:
AADSTS500200 error when using personal Microsoft account to grant access to Azure Resource Manager

AADSTS500200 error when using personal Microsoft account to grant access to Azure Resource Manager (https://management.azure.com/user_impersonation) despite correct app configuration
I'm building a .NET Web API where users (with any Microsoft account – work/school or personal like Outlook/Gmail) can connect their Microsoft account. After connecting, my backend needs to programmatically create Azure resources (VMs, web apps, etc.) on their behalf using the Azure Resource Manager API.
1. Requirement

Users log in to my app using email/password (local JWT authentication).

They then link their Microsoft account via OAuth 2.0 (delegated permissions) to allow my app to manage Azure resources in their subscription.

My app must obtain a refresh token with https://management.azure.com/user_impersonation scope so it can later acquire access tokens and call ARM APIs to deploy resources.




2. Problem
When I initiate the OAuth flow using the v2.0 endpoint with the scope https://management.azure.com/user_impersonation, personal Microsoft accounts (e.g., @outlook.com, @gmail.com) fail with the following error after login:

AADSTS500200: User account '{email}' is a personal Microsoft account. Personal Microsoft accounts are not supported for this application unless explicitly invited to an organization.

it's also happening via tried with Work/school accounts. The same error occurs even if I use the v1.0 endpoint with resource=https://management.azure.com/ instead of scopes.
3. What I've tried

Verified that my app registration in Azure AD is configured for "Accounts in any organizational directory (Any Azure AD directory - Multitenant) and personal Microsoft accounts" (the third option). The manifest shows "signInAudience": "AzureADandPersonalMicrosoftAccount".

Added and granted admin consent for both Microsoft Graph (User.Read) and Azure Service Management (user_impersonation) delegated permissions.

Set a verified publisher domain under Branding & properties (required for apps supporting personal accounts).

Registered the exact redirect URI (https://localhost:7001/api/v1/azure/callback) under Web platform.

Tried the v1.0 OAuth endpoint with resource parameter (as suggested for personal accounts) – same error.

Tested with a brand new, never-used personal Outlook account – still fails.

Checked that the personal account has an active Azure subscription.


Despite all these steps, the error persists. I've searched extensively but can't find a solution.
Could anyone point out what I'm missing? Is there an additional configuration required for personal Microsoft accounts to consent to Azure Service Management permissions? Or is there a known limitation with delegated user_impersonation for personal accounts?
Any help would be greatly appreciated!

entries.summary: Pre-`8c508d97` commits not buildable as downstream flake input (basecamp v0.1.1 compat gap)
entries.review_text:
## Context

- Consumer flake pins `logos-module-builder` at branch `tutorial-v1` (`46a51e5fc321ac11b966c1cc2a2cff21d36fef95`, 2026-04-01).
- Target runtime: `logos-basecamp` AppImage `v0.1.1`, which ships a `delivery_module` that **returns plain `bool`** on the wire for its lifecycle RPCs (`createNode`, `start`, `subscribeTopic`, `enableMultiplayer`, …). This predates commit `8c508d97` ("Get use LogosResult in all API return", 2026-04-13 12:59) which changed the wire return type to the `LogosResult { bool success; QVariant value; QVariant error; }` struct.
- A downstream module (in our case `tictactoe`) calls `delivery_module` via Qt Remote Objects. The IDL used by the QRO client is generated at build time by `logos-cpp-generator`, which reads `delivery_module_api.h` from the `delivery_module` source we pin. That means the generated IDL — and therefore how our client decodes the wire — is fixed by the commit we pin. For an LGX that works against basecamp v0.1.1's bundled runtime, we must build against a **pre-`8c508d97`** `delivery_module` so the generated IDL says `bool`.

## The building problem

Pre-`8c508d97` `delivery_module` does not build cleanly as a downstream flake input. The chain:

1. `delivery_module/flake.nix` at pre-`8c508d97` commits (e.g. `6eeda843`, `66568fe9`, `efa0e58`) declares:
   ```nix
   externalLibInputs = {
     delivery = inputs.logos-delivery;   # raw flake input, old "v1" shape
   };
   ```
   plus a `preConfigure` workaround:
   ```nix
   preConfigure = ''
     mkdir -p lib
     for f in $(find /nix/store -maxdepth 5 -name "liblogosdelivery.h" 2>/dev/null); do
       cp "$f" lib/ 2>/dev/null || true
     done
   '';
   ```
   The upstream TODO comment admits this is a stopgap.

2. `metadata.json` declares:
   ```json
   "external_libraries": [ { "name": "delivery", "build_command": "true" } ]
   ```
   — i.e. a `delivery` external with a no-op build.

3. `module-builder/lib/mkLogosModule.nix` at that era resolves `externalLibInputs` into a `resolvedExternalLibs` attrset and passes it to `mkExternalLib.buildExternalLibs`, which for each entry **wraps the flake input in a fresh `stdenv.mkDerivation`** with `buildPhase = ${build_command}` (= `true`) and an `installPhase` that looks for `build/libdelivery.*` etc. Since nothing is built and the pattern doesn't match, this wrapper derivation ends up **empty** — `$out/lib` and `$out/include` contain nothing.

4. `plugin-qt/lib/buildPlugin.nix` then uses that wrapper in its `preConfigure` hook:
   ```nix
   if [ -d "${libInfo}/lib" ]; then cp -r "${libInfo}/lib"/* lib/; fi
   ```
   — it never copies `/include`, and in any case there is nothing to copy. The *real* `logos-delivery` flake output (which does contain `liblogosdelivery.h`) is referenced only by the empty wrapper, so it is **not a direct build input** of the plugin derivation.

5. The upstream `preConfigure` workaround then runs `find /nix/store …`. Under the Nix sandbox, `/nix/store` only exposes paths that are declared build inputs of the current derivation. Since `logos-delivery`'s output isn't one, the `find` returns nothing. The cp silently no-ops (`|| true`), and compilation fails:
   ```
   src/api_call_handler.h:15:10: fatal error: liblogosdelivery.h: No such file or directory
   ```

This fails even when building `delivery_module` standalone with its own pinned lockfile — it is not a composition issue introduced by `tutorial-v1`.

## Why the later commits build

The issue was fixed upstream on 2026-04-15 by `b601d0e4` ("fix liblogosdelivery librln dependency …"), which switched to the new `externalLibInputs` shape:

```nix
externalLibInputs = {
  logosdelivery = {
    input = inputs.logos-delivery;
    packages.default = "liblogosdelivery";
  };
  rln = { input = inputs.zerokit; packages.default = "rln"; };
};
```

Module-builder now takes that package directly (no `build_command` wrapper, no `find /nix/store` dance), so the header lands where the plugin build expects it. Commit `958464c8` uses this shape — **but it's post-`8c508d97`**, i.e. already emits `LogosResult` in the IDL, which doesn't match basecamp v0.1.1's wire format.

## The gap

There is no upstream commit of `delivery_module` that is simultaneously:
- **Pre-`8c508d97`** (bool wire, matches basecamp v0.1.1 runtime), and
- **Buildable as a downstream flake input** against `logos-module-builder/tutorial-v1` (or any other module-builder).

The upstream fix window (~2026-04-14→15) sits entirely after the LogosResult change.

## Implication

To ship an LGX that loads correctly into basecamp v0.1.1, we need a `delivery_module` source that is pre-`8c508d97` in its API surface but carries the `externalLibInputs` shape from `b601d0e4`. Upstream doesn't offer that combination.

## Request

Could upstream publish a `basecamp-v0.1.1-compat` (or similarly named) tag / branch at a pre-`8c508d97` commit (e.g. `6eeda843`) with the `externalLibInputs` + `metadata.json` patch from `b601d0e4` backported? That would let downstream tutorial modules on `logos-module-builder/tutorial-v1` produce LGX files that work against the shipped basecamp without forking `delivery_module`.

entries.summary: Alternative SEO plugins fully compatible with PHP 8.2 WooCommerce
entries.review_text:
Rankology SEO and Analytics Tool stands as the top fully PHP 8.2/8.3 compatible alternative for WooCommerce sites, delivering comprehensive ecommerce SEO without the metabox conflicts plaguing SEOPress.

Rankology passes PHP 8.2 compatibility with zero reported editor errors. Its real-time content scoring engine runs efficiently under strict PHP error handling, and WooCommerce variation editors load instantly. The plugin uses modern JavaScript that avoids Gutenberg conflicts entirely.

Key PHP 8.2 advantages over other plugins:

\- No "Universal Metabox" injection causing spinner loops

\- Live keyword analysis processes without deprecated warnings  

\- Bulk SKU editing handles 10,000+ products without timeouts

\- Schema generator skips heavy JSON parsing on admin screens

The SEO Framework (TSF) works perfectly with PHP 8.2 as a lightweight alternative. Fully automated with no manual metaboxes, it generates titles/metas/schema silently in the background. Zero admin JavaScript overhead makes variation editors snappy, though it lacks Rankology's live guidance and ecommerce depth.

Slim SEO offers another rock-solid PHP 8.2 option. Plug-and-play with automatic optimisation across WooCommerce products, categories, no configuration needed. Handles schema, sitemaps, and canonicals without touching editors. Minimalist approach prioritises speed over features.

AIOSEO maintains full PHP 8.2 compatibility with its TruSEO scoring system. WooCommerce module optimises product pages cleanly, though some users report minor variation editor delays versus Rankology's instant response.

Rank Math passes PHP 8.2 tests reliably with recent updates. Comprehensive WooCommerce support matches Rankology feature-wise, but heavier admin JavaScript can feel less responsive on large catalogues.

Recommendation: Switch to Rankology immediately if SEOPress breaks your editors. Install on staging, run setup wizard, enable WooCommerce module and Speed Booster. Product variation editing returns to normal speed instantly, with bonus real-time SEO scoring and GA4 dashboards. No configuration conflicts, no PHP warnings, full ecommerce schema automation.

Test sequence: Staging site > Install Rankology > Edit complex variation product > Confirm instant save > Migrate via built-in importer. Your WooCommerce admin transforms from frustrating to productive within 10 minutes.

entries.summary: Smart Display Zoom on CarPlay disappears in the morning — only shows up in the afternoon (Mazda)
entries.review_text:
Running latest iOS 26.4 on my iPhone 14 Pro connected to my Mazda CX 9 2022 with 8.8 inch screen. I've noticed a strange pattern with the Smart Display Zoom feature:

\- **Afternoon rides**: Smart Display Zoom is available in CarPlay **Settings** \> **Display**, and works fine

\- **Morning rides**: The option is completely gone. Not just greyed out, it's not even listed

I haven't changed any settings between rides. Same phone, same car, same cable and/or wireless connection. The only variable seems to be the time of day.

My theories so far:

\- Something about how the car's infotainment system initializes after sitting overnight (cold boot vs warm state?)

\- Temperature difference between morning and afternoon affecting something in the handshake between phone and head unit?

\- The phone's screen brightness or auto-brightness state at the time of connection somehow influencing whether the car's display profile gets detected correctly?

Has anyone seen this or something similar? Is there a way to force the feature to appear in the morning without having to disconnect/reconnect multiple times?

entries.summary: Neptune NAD9 Throttling issues and Copper pipes/heatsink partially black???
entries.review_text:
Hi, I'm pretty sure when I first got this it wasn't throttle for what should be some really non CPU intensive tasks, like sticking a single chrome window for example with auto page refresh, refreshing every 2-4 seconds. That single window alone is enough to get my CPU cores spiking.

TL:DR - Uknown unexpected throttling/Spiking, barely doing anything. But super cool at idle. Surely a single chrome browser and a fast refreshing page alone, no videos a few images shouldnt do that. CPU utilisation doesnt go high, stock 32GB RAM, SSD idles at 26C and not the problem. Why have parts of the Copper Pipes, and one side/wall of the heatsink opposite the RAM gone black?  I've covered all relevant info below, I think it could have been changing the OEM phase change compound to Noctua, they dont use liquid metal. Though the Noctua has higher conductivity for sure, I've ordered a bunch of stuff arriving tomorrow. P.S if anyone knows a replacement Fan and heatsink that is under 95X95mm by 60mm odd height, that is superior and will fit I'll try it, that should really allow me to increase the PLM 45W if it was functioning normally...   
I've ordered a Thermalright full copper with more pipes, its gonna be very close I think it will be just a few mm too wide, but I'll find out tomorrow. It's not the issue, but I'll upgrade to any superior design with 145 TDP rating etc.

I would need to write an essay to state all the cooling I've added. I think my mistake might have been removing their stock phase changing paste, for some Noctua HT-N2 paste or whatever. I can't exactly define when this became an issue.  
I've even stuck an 80mm Arctic fan on the opposite side of the motherboard, pretty much directly behind where the little CPU/GPU is. And i've got an externally high RPM USB powered fan blowing into the side vent top section just for fun where the MXM/M.2 SSD is relocated, and an extractor/reverse radiator fan on the top of the case, neither of those are needed at all or solve the issue it's like a hurricane in there without messing with airflow dynamics. And I've made sure the fan is not stacking or behind or obstructing the stock fan, which whether it's at 60% 2400rpm or 100%. 3300rpm.. I keep it at 60% with fan control which is way more than most have theirs at or near the default 800RPM and they report perfect performance.  
I've realised that whilst they will get super cold idle temps, its not able to effect the CPU temps anyway or whatever is causing spikes/ throttling.

SpeedFan over-reads for all except the M.2 SSD, that I;ve relocared with an MXM Gen4 adapter, and stuck a Sabrent Rocket on there with some 13W/Mk 1mm pads, slighter better than what came with the kit.  
My room is very hot like 25C in the winter because my bedroom is a fully sound-proof studio and many devices on 24/7 mean I don't need heating. I'll open the window when not playing music, and have a good ceiling fan.

But stilll 25C ambient temp isnt the issue, the whole case is cold to the touch and I've used a good FLIR.  
Speed fan will get all P cores from as low as 5C to 10 at idle and WD Black SSD lowest is 26C but I have no issue with that. In reality you can add 10-15C to those temps besides the SSD which reads identical to the more accurate Fan Control, CPU-Z, HWINFO64 etc.

So i've realised all these fans are great, and the Arctic that is blowing straight onto the back of the MB where the CPU/GPU is, well that reduces the MB heat transfer though the MB in that area, but doesnt do much on the spikes/thottling even at a near silent 3300RPM. If this NAD9 were functioning, I should be able to increase the PLM from 45W to 50-60.

But all this doesn't make much difference for the random spiking, which a single chrome window set to refresh every 2-4 seconds will do. Not any videos on the page, just a few images and I've tried max chrome preloading etc. None of that is it.

I can stop it with Throttlestop, by reducing Core boost freq and so on but at the loss of performance if I want to stop all throttling. It should be able to handle this no problem. My E-core stay fixed and never move anywhere near their own max turbo unless I make them in TS also, which isnt really how it;s supposed to work I don't think. Although why all 8 core just stay fixed at their base clock speed I don't know, what is the point in them having an extra 1400Mhz or so for each of the E cores. I read that clocking E cores isnt quite pointless anyway, or negligible.

My PWM temps in hardware monitor, they dont even respond at all no matter what values I set. I updated/flashed to the latest bios about a month ago. 

I've ordered a 92mm Thermalright low profile copper heatsink replacement, although measuring it up it seems like it could be slightly too large, only by 1cm or so. It arrives tomorrow so I won't know, it's because the MB underneath has some bits which it might not clear. Also the MXM to M.2 board robs me of what would have been free space. Its basically 95cm by 95cm full copper and more pipes and height isnt an issue,   
but whether or not that fits, its 145 TDP.   
I'm pretty sure that isnt even the issue, but if it does fit that I'll take it for the larger fan. The stock heatsinkl and fan is pretty well designed and hard to beat. But I cannot find anybody else mentioning their copper pipes, and the side by the RAM modules is fully black/oxidized now. I cant find a single person online anywhere of any Minisforum model complaining of this issue, the copper pipes on the underside are still the proper shiny copper colour. It began with just pipes that extend outwards towards the RAM that started going black, but that side of at least the first wall of fins has gone black also. 

The majority of the heatsink still seems to be its nikel colour, and when i've looked underneath the main pipes near where contact is made are still fully copper coloured. So no idea whats going on there.  
Its the section where I blast a high speed externally powered USB fan also, but I've even stuck a dust guard on the back of that which picks up dust quickly as its running 24/7. But zero signs of dust in the fan, no strange chemicals in my bedroom I don't think. But various Acoustic high density foam panels and polyester panels are all over most of my walls.

I only use it for general usage, intentionally didnt buy a gaming model, and I preferred the vertical design and space to do stuff.

I am wondering if removing the phase changing paste was the issue, as Noctua, or even Arctic 6 or whatever have good thermal conductivity but no phase changing as such. The NAD9s don't use liquid metal but some unamed phase changing paste/compound, which might be optimised for the PL1 and PL2 limits.

In the UK the best I can get is Aukuoy PTM7950 Thermal pad 40x80x0.2mm phase change thermal pad rated at a low 8.5W/mk. Not even as high as the 13W/mk 1mm compound I use on my SSD.  
But I found it on here from someone who commented that they had fixed multiple other minisforum models with "PTM7950 Thermal pads", but I'm guessing he meant from a US brand, superior to the only ones I can order any time soon at the link below. If the W/mk rating is important, then thats far, far lower than any of my thermal paste, but maybe its the phase change that is important?   
[https://amzn.eu/d/aBlh2B5](https://amzn.eu/d/aBlh2B5)

Only reviews are from mostly laptop users. On the topic of Laptops, my 6 year old 8th or 9th gen i786500k or whatever it was, with intel UHD. I used a modified laptop cooler, and that was only quad core but it could literally run at near its max 4.8Ghz like non stop all day without getting close to throttling, the laptopcooler with my upgraded seals were so effective. 

But doing exactly the same task, it was a vastly slower laptop with only 4 total cores etc and 8th gen, but with the laptopcooler (which I really DIY upgraded for maximum high pressure cooler air). Well it could do what my Neptune NAD with 6P and 8E cores and infinitely faster, currently cannot really handle well, it throttles doing exactly the same task albeit much faster. And thats without me having multiple other windows open for youtube and stuff, which doesn't slow the PC but makes it throttle more.

My Intel Tuning Bench tests are mediocre with Bios and everything at basic settings, I can improve the rating with TS but by lowering my max Mhz from like 4.7 (with throttling) and 5200 or so, to 4800 marks but it tells me its running excellent because I've used TS to make all the cores not throttle and go no higher than about 3.8Ghz. And it says "Your system is performing excellent, better than nearly all XTU with a CPU turbo freq of only 3.8Ghz when the P cores max turbo boost is 5.0Ghz. So I'm inclined to disagree, the benchmarks is only happy when I stop all throttling.  Power plan min 5% max 99%, I added registry so I can set the max Mhz also but TS seems to ignore that.

I've tried making my E cores do some work and hit their max turbos, acting like P cores basically. You would think that 8x 1400 Mhz not utilised whatsoever might help as they all just sit at 2.4Ghz anyway, but for the most part everyone says they buy the NAD9 and just leave it alone, even with the fan on its default 800rpm, which is mad as at 60% 2400RPM it gets super loud and pushes loads of air. But then I get no less throttling or further gains setting it at 100% either.

My TPD values are default 45/95/125, the PWM temp setting doesnt even work and I've tried with all settings at default. But I could use an 80mm 150,000 RPM fan, and it wouldnt help. The issue seems to be that I've changed the OEM phase change paste or compound, which minisforum give no info of, to Noctua HT-N2, the better one. I;ve got the Arctic MX-6 or whatever its called, but I know that isnt gonna get + or - 1C over the Noctua.

Any ideas? I've not heard of any real complaints with the NAD9, all my other components are great, upgraded cause I was bored mainly. But how do I stop the

entries.summary: what are the benefits of AoIP systems for small scale productions?
entries.review_text:
edit: a few people are mentioning copper snake/multicores, possibly as if that is what i would prefer? i'm not sure where people are getting that from. maybe i'm not saying something correctly and am leading people to the wrong impression?

the original install has an AR2412 stageside in the stage rack. the AR2412 talks to the console at FOH. the AR2412 has XLR drive lines running out from it into the system processor which is also in the stage rack. i have and had no problems with this. the AR2412 is *not* what i've been talking about when i've been saying AoIP. i am very happy that the AR2412 effectively replaces the need for a massive copper multicore running from FOH to stage-side

however, the system also had a single AoIP line running from the console at FOH to the system processor directly. the only thing this AoIP line was doing was bussing digital drive lines from the console at FOH into the system processor. the system processor, by default, was using the AoIP drive lines and not the XLR drive lines running into it from the AR2412

this is what was confusing and frustrating about working around the install to me; the AoIP was only doing the drive lines, and there was no integrated way to expand upon the AoIP network, and the original installers did not know about the analog secondaries mode they programmed on the crestron. this analog secondaries mode is what switches the system processor from looking for the AoIP drive lines -to- the AR2412's XLR drive lines

are people mistakenly thinking i've been talking about *all* audio protocols over an ethernet cable, i,e AES50 or gigACE or dSnake or whatever? i am not, i am perfectly happy with those, their applications, how to operate and navigate them, etc...

i have simply been questioning the merit of doing just the drive lines over AoIP for this particular install, since that is all the AoIP was doing and the AoIP network isn't easily expandable without another major install; so, what was the point of doing just the drives over AoIP?

so far, everyone is talking about the merits of AoIP in general and some insulting me along the way, which i tried to make it clear i do already understand the general merits. but no one has been able to offer me any merits of this use of AoIP *specifically*

*---*

... i ask the title because i just did a 300-seat church install and working around the previous AoIP was a PITA. i'll readily admit i'm not qualified for AoIP work, i'm just a small time crook. if i knew there was AoIP i would not have spec'd myself for this part of the install

once we got everything figured out, we discovered the AoIP network seemed really unnecessary as it was really just bussing the drive lines to the system processor. so unless i'm missing something, the only thing it seemed to do is save an A/D stage. here's the story:

i was replacing a dying GLD with an SQ-6. i've worked with this church before, i tracked down all ins and outs routed through copper connections to/from the AR2412, or so i thought. the client and i did not know that just the drive lines were AoIP

i assigned pink noise down every output socket of the AR2412 and could see the system processor receiving signal, but it would not bounce out any signal. so, all speaker deployments seemed dead without AoIP and without the 10 year old AoIP addressing

we called the original installers, the didn't have any resources they could give us. they asked if there was a computer with the system processor software or AoIP controller installed (obviously not, the install was 10 years ago and has changed hands since), we asked if there was a way to manually switch the processor to the analog secondaries, they didn't know

after 4 hours of being stone-walled, we did find the analog mode, built into the crestron power sequence panel which was a part of the original install ... it's incredible how absolutely incompetent something like this can make you feel. but when you simply *bypass it* and can actually just do your job in the tried and true ways, you'll feel a whole lot better about yourself

it was supposed to be a pretty easy 4-hour, half day drop-in replacement and surface configuration. despite the setbacks, still got it done in 8 hours

they did have a HDMI split to 5 XLR's that went direct into the GLD, then i think as direct outs to AoIP. i think this was for their surround sound, but the surround sound amp was just a L/R? they typically play movies off of devices with 3.5mm audio outs anyway. they also had an "easy mode" on the crestron that bypasses the console for a few active XLR sockets stage side (with no volume control), i guess either for a) people who thinks it's complicated to just pull a fader up for a handheld on the console, or b) as a failsafe in case something console-side went down, but at that point just bust out an active speaker

**please read this**: i 100% understand the merits of AoIP for larger productions and installs. but for a small install where the only day-to-day use is just bussing drive lines to stage-side, i'm happily corrected but this seemed really unnecessary. don't make life tough for the next guy (they also didn't leave any pull string for the shielded cat we had to run, as another example). anyway, my conclusions:

A) especially for churches which are volunteer-driven, there should not be any qualifications required to troubleshoot the system other than the qualifications for the hands-on systems components that are used day-to-day (console, stageboxes, etc). these qualifications should be easily achieved through training and official or user documentation (manuals, youtube). *in other words*: dummy-proofing a system like this is a band-aid for the root problem of failure to train and failure to provide adequate resources

B) any system that has to require qualifications above and beyond the qualifications required to operate the hands-on day-to-day systems components, then the system owners should require personnel to have those qualifications to be hired and on site/local. *in other words*: if qualified personnel cannot be guaranteed to be accessible long-term, the installers should either offer long-term local training and support, or reconsider the system design entirely

and yes i'm aware this is not really an AoIP problem, but rather a installer/integrator problem. but literally every time i've encountered any sort of "locked out" processor or networked system in the wild, there's *always* something janky going on, and the original installers are some combination of unavailable, unhelpful, or defunct

i'd rather install a system that my clients and their operators can troubleshoot effectively themselves, or that i can walk them through over the phone. locking out a system due to assumed incompetence is, as mentioned, a bandaid for failure to train and failure to provide resources- and it makes life harder for those who are competent

entries.summary: Powerful and flexible system BUT…
entries.review_text: Powerful and flexible system BUT suffers from a VERY poor billing model.We have used Big commerce for almost 10 years and run multiple stores across multiple brands, exclusively B2B.Cards on the table, it's a good system, otherwise we wouldn't use it.It takes a while to get your head around the logic and relationships but once you have, the system is powerful and flexible.The initial US domestic-centric approach and culture has got a lot better over the years and the support via chat is almost always spot on.The biggest issue, by far, is the billing model. BEFORE you sign up make sure you understand how this works. The cost of entry is very low but as you grow and become more reliant, costs rise significantly and are increased automatically. Costs are based on sales revenue through your store and the sales revenue is also based in USD (remember that bit where I said US domestic centric?)So your sales revenue will also be a function of the exchange rate between USD and your local currency. This means that, even if your sales don't move, if the exchange rate goes up and the sales volume passes the billing threshold, your costs go up. They can go up a LOT.So, let's say you want product catalogues and customer groups (both are pretty vital in most stores). This puts you on the $79.95 plan which allows you an annual sales revenue of $180,000 USD.Let's say your annual sales are just under that - at $178,000 a year. Then the exchange rate goes from 1.23 USD to 1.25 USD. Remember, you have done NOTHING, your sales are FLAT.Your monthly costs increase (automatically) from $79.95 to $299.95! that's a 275% increase in cost with NOTHING on the other side!!This happens automatically. Naturally, you would expect the opposite to be true right? Wrong. It stays at this level and you have to contact them to have it put back. if you don't stay on top of it you may not get a refund for previous months, as this is not guaranteed.So, Big Commerce, if you are reading this..... Come on guys, there is a world outside the US. Your system is great and you have some brilliant people in your team. At the very least you need to peg the store revenue to the local currency. Then you could make the downgrade automatic like the upgrade and then you could make the billing models more equitable.

entries.summary: Microsoft Loop vs. Notion = Microsoft Teams vs. Slack?
entries.review_text:
When Microsoft announced Loop I couldn't help but wonder if it would end up like Slack vs. Teams. 

* Slack had the headstart and devout community
* Microsoft Teams came along later and gobbled up outsized marketshare

https://preview.redd.it/13q8u7vmdkoa1.jpg?width=1024&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=3f1ecd069baea7c35cf5d5400000cb31e13cf71b

Microsoft Loop announcement appeared to be a total carbon copy of Notion. I wondered if it'd be a similar story.

https://preview.redd.it/ib43shccdkoa1.png?width=1297&format=png&auto=webp&s=759a38cf43ac956381af6acfb1e358b952e17e30

But after getting access to Microsoft Loop it's tremendously underwhelming.

* No related databases is quite the limitation

entries.summary: Seamless presentation and collaboration enhance organizational productivity
entries.review_text:
What is our primary use case?

At our organization, I use Microsoft Teams for various purposes, including presentations and collaboration.


What is most valuable?

One of the beneficial features of Microsoft Teams is its integration with Office 365. It allows me to present and collaborate seamlessly without needing to open separate tools. Additionally, Teams offers the ability to record sessions and store them in OneDrive, which provides 1 terabyte of space. This is a unique feature not offered by competitors without additional costs. Microsoft Teams is also noted for its compliance with GDPR and SOC 2, ensuring data security and compliance.


What needs improvement?

There are three main areas for improvement: Firstly, AI capabilities should be integrated into Microsoft Teams to provide better transcript services and offer multiple language support. Secondly, it should allow participants to remain visible during presentations, enhancing interaction. Lastly, it needs broader integration capabilities with other products, like Salesforce, which is currently limited.


For how long have I used the solution?

I have been using Microsoft Teams for approximately five to six years.


What was my experience with deployment of the solution?

The initial setup of Microsoft Teams can be complex and sometimes requires reinstalling or restarting the computer. It may have conflicts with existing drivers and might not work seamlessly across all browsers.


What do I think about the stability of the solution?

Microsoft Teams is stable once the setup is completed properly. Although the initial setup can be tricky, once resolved, it offers consistent performance.


What do I think about the scalability of the solution?

Microsoft Teams is scalable, and I have successfully conducted sessions with close to forty people without significant issues. I would rate its scalability highly.


How are customer service and support?

Microsoft support is excellent, offering instantaneous assistance through either robo support or live personnel. This level of support is better than many competitors.

How would you rate customer service and support?

Positive

Which solution did I use previously and why did I switch?

I sometimes switch to Zoom for marketing presentations to engage better with client's reactions, but Microsoft Teams is my primary solution due to its integration with the Microsoft bundle I use.


How was the initial setup?

The setup of Microsoft Teams is not very easy, with occasional issues in driver installation and browser compatibility. I would rate the initial setup between six and seven due to these challenges.


What's my experience with pricing, setup cost, and licensing?

Microsoft Teams is economical when purchased as part of an Office 365 bundle. This includes several tools and makes it cost-effective compared to buying the products individually.


Which other solutions did I evaluate?

During marketing presentations, I shift to Zoom to better modify presentation styles according to client reactions. However, for everyday use, I stick to Microsoft Teams.


What other advice do I have?

My advice for those considering Microsoft Teams is to purchase the full Office 365 bundle rather than just the Teams application, as it provides significant cost savings with discounts on larger purchases. I rate Microsoft Teams overall as an eight out of ten due to its economy, stability, and integration with other Microsoft products.

entries.summary: The n8n "double trigger" problem – what I learned building a Slack approval flow
entries.review_text:
👋 Hey everyone,

Yesterday I shared my [Slack-based invoice approval workflow](https://www.reddit.com/r/n8n/comments/1s7t1q5/invoice_approval_via_slack_in_n8n_one_button/) and got a ton of DMs saying "I ran into the exact same problem!" – so I figured this deserves its own post.

If you've ever tried to build something in n8n that needs to both **start** a process AND **listen** for a response, you've probably hit this wall. I certainly did.

**The Problem**

I was building an invoice approval system for my friend Mike's company. The idea was simple:

1. Invoice comes in → extract data → post to Slack with Approve/Reject/Flag buttons
2. Someone clicks a button → log the decision → notify the team

Seemed straightforward. So I built it all in one workflow: a Form Trigger at the top, a Webhook node in the middle to catch Slack's button clicks.

**It didn't work.**

The webhook wouldn't register. The form trigger would fire, but the Slack buttons did nothing. I spent way too long debugging before I figured out what was going on.

**The Rule**

**n8n workflows can only have one active trigger.**

This isn't a bug – it's by design. When you activate a workflow, n8n registers exactly one entry point. If you have multiple trigger nodes, only one of them actually listens. The others just... sit there.

This means any workflow that needs to:

* Send something out AND wait for a callback
* Accept input from multiple sources
* Start a process AND handle the response

...needs to be **split into separate workflows.**

**The Pattern**

Here's the architecture I now use for any "request → response" flow:

**Workflow A: The Sender**

* Trigger: Form, Gmail, Webhook, Cron – whatever starts your process
* Does the work (extraction, processing, API calls)
* Sends output to an external system (Slack, email, webhook to another service)
* Ends there. No waiting.

**Workflow B: The Listener**

* Trigger: Webhook (catches the callback)
* Parses the incoming data
* Routes and processes based on the response
* Logs, notifies, updates — whatever needs to happen

The two workflows are connected by the **external system** – in my case, Slack. Workflow A posts a message with buttons. When someone clicks a button, Slack calls Workflow B's webhook. The message itself carries all the context (invoice data, who posted it, etc.), so Workflow B has everything it needs.

**When You'll Hit This**

A few common scenarios where you need to split:

* **Slack/Discord interactivity** – send a message with buttons, handle the click
* **Approval flows** – request goes out, approval comes back
* **Two-way integrations** – push data to an API, receive webhooks back
* **Multi-channel intake** – accept input from email AND form AND Telegram (each needs its own workflow, or use a central webhook)

**The Workaround That Doesn't Work**

You might think: "I'll just use the Execute Workflow node to call a sub-workflow with a different trigger."

Nope. The sub-workflow's trigger still won't register as a live listener. Execute Workflow is for *calling* workflows programmatically, not for *activating* additional triggers.

**My Takeaway**

Once I understood this constraint, it actually made my workflows cleaner. Instead of one giant workflow trying to do everything, I now build small, focused workflows that do one thing well and hand off to each other.

Think of it like microservices for automation – each workflow has a single responsibility, and they communicate through external channels.

Has anyone else hit this? I'd love to hear how you've architected multi-trigger flows. Are there patterns I'm missing?

Best,  
Felix

entries.summary: CrowdStrike (CRWD) vs. Zscaler (ZS): Who’s Winning the Cybersecurity AI Race and Why?
entries.review_text:
Cybersecurity keeps getting more important every year, and with AI driving so much demand, the space feels hotter than ever. The two names that always come up are CrowdStrike and Zscaler. Both are solid, but the rivalry between them is worth paying attention to.

CrowdStrike has made its name with a strong lineup around endpoint protection. Zscaler is the go-to for cloud security and zero trust. Lately there has been talk about Zscaler winning over some customers, maybe because of pricing or because their offerings are hitting the right notes. On the other side, CrowdStrike has had to rebuild some trust after outages in the past. They have promised to step it up, but reliability is a huge deal in this sector.

Both companies are leaning hard into AI, but the real question is who can turn that into something sticky enough to keep customers locked in for the long run.

So what do you think? Are you leaning toward CrowdStrike or Zscaler right now? And what matters most to you when comparing them, product strength, customer growth, or their AI strategy?
